scenarios.title: Dual Relationship Dilemma
scenarios.scenario: You are a social worker at a community mental health center in a small rural town. Your new client, Maria (age 34), arrives for her intake appointment. You recognize her as the parent of a child in your daughter's soccer team. Maria discloses she is experiencing severe depression and marital conflict. She specifically requested you because she heard you are 'the best therapist in town.' The nearest alternative provider is 45 miles away, and Maria lacks reliable transportation.
scenarios.writing_prompt: Write a comprehensive ethical analysis addressing: (1) Identify the ethical dilemma and relevant NASW Code of Ethics sections, (2) Discuss the risks and benefits of accepting this client, (3) Propose a plan of action with rationale, (4) Document how you would address boundaries if you accept the case.

identification.criteria: Correctly identifies dual relationship, cites NASW 1.06(c), discusses rural practice considerations

analysis.criteria: Weighs benefits (access to care, client preference) against risks (compromised objectivity, community dynamics)

action_plan.criteria: Proposes concrete steps: consultation, informed consent about dual relationship, boundary agreement, supervision plan, referral options

documentation.criteria: Professional writing, proper social work terminology, addresses ongoing boundary monitoring

model_response_key_points: Acknowledge the ethical tension between client self-determination and professional boundaries
Reference NASW Code of Ethics Section 1.06(c) on dual relationships
Consider the unique challenges of rural social work practice
Discuss informed consent regarding the dual relationship
Outline specific boundary management strategies
Include a plan for ongoing supervision and consultation
Consider documentation requirements
scenarios.tips: On the licensing exam, rural practice scenarios frequently test your ability to balance competing ethical principles. Remember that not all dual relationships are prohibited — the key is whether the relationship could cause harm.

scenarios.title: Confidentiality and Duty to Warn
scenarios.scenario: During a routine session, your client James (age 28) becomes agitated and states: 'If my ex-girlfriend doesn't stop seeing that guy, something bad is going to happen. I've been watching their apartment. I know his schedule. He parks in the same spot every night.' When you probe further, James says, 'I'm not going to do anything stupid, I just want to scare him.' James has a history of assault charges (dismissed) and was previously mandated for anger management. He denies owning weapons but has made vague threats before that were not acted upon.
scenarios.writing_prompt: Write a detailed clinical response that includes: (1) A risk assessment based on the information provided, (2) Your legal and ethical obligations in this scenario, (3) The specific steps you would take immediately and in follow-up, (4) How you would document this session, (5) How you would address this with James therapeutically.

risk_assessment.criteria: Identifies specific risk factors: history of violence, surveillance behavior, identified victim, escalating language

legal_ethical.criteria: References Tarasoff, state duty-to-warn laws, NASW 1.07(c), discusses when confidentiality must be broken

action_steps.criteria: Immediate: safety assessment, consultation with supervisor, contact identified victim/law enforcement. Follow-up: safety plan, treatment modification

documentation.criteria: Detailed progress note including exact quotes, risk assessment rationale, actions taken, consultation obtained

therapeutic.criteria: Addresses maintaining therapeutic alliance while fulfilling duty, discusses transparency with client about limits

model_response_key_points: Conduct structured risk assessment (e.g., history of violence, specificity of threat, means, opportunity)
Reference Tarasoff v. Regents of the University of California
Identify duty to warn/protect under state law
Consult with supervisor before taking action
Notify identifiable potential victim and/or law enforcement
Document thoroughly with direct quotes where possible
Address with client transparently about limits of confidentiality
Modify treatment plan to address anger and threat behaviors
scenarios.tips: Licensing exams almost always include a duty-to-warn question. Remember: the threat must be specific and credible, with an identifiable victim. When in doubt, always err on the side of protecting potential victims.

scenarios.title: Biopsychosocial Assessment — Adolescent in Foster Care
scenarios.scenario: Jaylen is a 15-year-old African American male who has been in foster care for 3 years following removal due to parental substance abuse and neglect. He is currently in his fourth foster placement. His current foster parents report increasing behavioral problems including truancy, defiance, and suspected marijuana use. Jaylen's school reports declining grades (from B average to failing three classes), frequent fights, and social withdrawal. Jaylen tells you he 'doesn't care about anything anymore' and that 'nobody wants me anyway.' He has a history of ADHD (diagnosed at age 8) and was previously prescribed Adderall, which he stopped taking after his third placement change. Jaylen enjoys basketball and drawing but has stopped participating in both. His biological mother is currently in a residential treatment program and has begun supervised visits.
scenarios.writing_prompt: Complete a comprehensive biopsychosocial assessment for Jaylen. Include: (1) Biological factors, (2) Psychological factors, (3) Social/environmental factors, (4) Strengths assessment, (5) DSM-5 diagnostic considerations, (6) Treatment recommendations with prioritization, (7) Goals for the case plan.

biological.criteria: ADHD history, medication discontinuation, substance use screening, developmental health needs, nutrition/sleep assessment

psychological.criteria: Trauma history, attachment disruption, grief/loss, identity development, potential depression screening, hopelessness statements

social.criteria: Placement instability, school functioning, peer relationships, racial identity, cultural factors, biological family connections, foster family dynamics

strengths.criteria: Basketball, artistic ability, prior academic success, mother's engagement in treatment, resilience

diagnostic.criteria: ADHD pre-existing, rule out MDD, PTSD, adjustment disorder, substance use disorder; consider trauma lens vs pathologizing

treatment.criteria: Prioritized recommendations: safety/stability first, then trauma processing, medication evaluation, school supports, family therapy, mentoring, cultural connections

model_response_key_points: Use a trauma-informed lens throughout the assessment
Address each domain thoroughly with specific details from the scenario
Identify strengths explicitly — not just deficits
Consider racial identity development and cultural factors
Be cautious about over-pathologizing normative responses to trauma
Prioritize placement stability and relational consistency
Include measurable, time-specific goals
Reference evidence-based practices (TF-CBT, EMDR, etc.)
scenarios.tips: On the licensing exam, biopsychosocial assessments test your ability to think systemically. Always consider the interaction between biological, psychological, and social factors rather than treating them as isolated domains.

scenarios.title: SOAP Note — Substance Abuse Session
scenarios.scenario: You are a social worker at an outpatient substance abuse treatment center. You just completed a session with David (age 42), who is in his 6th week of treatment for alcohol use disorder. David reports he attended 5 of 7 AA meetings this week but had a 'slip' on Saturday when he drank two beers at a family barbecue. He states he felt pressured by his brother and 'didn't want to make a scene.' David expresses guilt and frustration, saying 'I ruined six weeks of sobriety.' He was tearful during the session. You worked with David on cognitive restructuring around his all-or-nothing thinking and helped him develop a relapse prevention plan for upcoming family events. David agreed to call his sponsor before the next family gathering and to practice refusal skills. He appeared more hopeful by the end of the session.
scenarios.writing_prompt: Write a complete SOAP note for this session following the standard format: Subjective (client's self-report), Objective (clinician observations and measurable data), Assessment (clinical analysis and progress evaluation), Plan (next steps and interventions).

subjective.criteria: Client's own words about the week, AA attendance, the lapse, feelings about it, family pressure. Uses direct quotes appropriately.

objective.criteria: Observable data: affect (tearful, then more hopeful), session attendance, AA meetings attended (5/7), specific lapse details (2 beers, Saturday), behavioral observations

assessment.criteria: Clinical analysis: distinguishes lapse from relapse, identifies cognitive distortions (all-or-nothing thinking), evaluates overall treatment progress, identifies triggers (family pressure)

plan.criteria: Specific next steps: continue weekly sessions, sponsor contact plan, refusal skills practice, address family dynamics, consider family session, next session date

model_response_key_points: Distinguish between a lapse (slip) and a full relapse
Use the client's exact language in the Subjective section
Include specific measurable data in the Objective section
Demonstrate clinical reasoning in the Assessment
Make the Plan specific and actionable
Maintain non-judgmental, strength-based language throughout
scenarios.tips: SOAP notes are one of the most commonly tested documentation formats. Keep each section distinct — avoid mixing subjective and objective data. The Assessment is where you show clinical reasoning, not just summarize.

scenarios.title: Suicide Risk Assessment
scenarios.scenario: You receive a call from the front desk that a walk-in client is requesting to see someone immediately. Sarah (age 22, college senior) appears disheveled, has been crying, and is clutching her phone. She tells you that her fiancé ended their engagement last night via text message. She says, 'I can't do this anymore. There's no point. Everyone leaves me.' When asked directly about suicidal thoughts, she says, 'I thought about driving into a bridge abutment on the way here, but I came here instead.' She reports she has not eaten or slept in 24 hours. Sarah has a history of one previous suicide attempt (overdose at age 17) and a diagnosis of Borderline Personality Disorder. She is currently prescribed Prozac but admits she stopped taking it 'a few weeks ago.' She lives alone and states her family is 'not an option' for support. She does have a close friend who 'doesn't know what's going on.'
scenarios.writing_prompt: Write a complete crisis intervention response including: (1) Structured suicide risk assessment identifying all risk and protective factors, (2) Risk level determination with rationale, (3) A detailed safety plan, (4) Immediate intervention steps, (5) Documentation of the encounter, (6) Follow-up plan.

risk_assessment.criteria: Identifies: ideation with plan (bridge), intent (came for help = ambivalence), prior attempt, discontinued medication, BPD diagnosis, acute loss, isolation, hopelessness. Protective: help-seeking, ambivalence, friend exists

risk_level.criteria: Correctly identifies as high risk with specific rationale; discusses whether involuntary hospitalization is warranted

safety_plan.criteria: Follows Stanley-Brown Safety Plan format: warning signs, coping strategies, social contacts, professional contacts, means restriction, reasons for living

intervention.criteria: Immediate: ensure physical safety, assess medical needs (no food/sleep), contact prescriber about medication, engage support system (friend with consent), determine level of care

documentation.criteria: Thorough documentation of assessment, rationale for clinical decisions, client statements in quotes, plan with timeline

model_response_key_points: Use a structured assessment tool (Columbia, SAD PERSONS, etc.)
Identify both risk factors AND protective factors
Note that help-seeking behavior is a significant protective factor
Address medication discontinuation as urgent medical concern
Create a specific, detailed safety plan with the client
Address lethal means (driving/bridge access)
Engage natural supports with client's consent
Document clinical decision-making process
Plan for follow-up within 24-48 hours
scenarios.tips: Suicide assessment is one of the MOST tested topics on licensing exams. Always assess: ideation, plan, intent, means, timeline, and protective factors. When a client comes to you voluntarily, that is always a protective factor worth noting.

scenarios.title: Applying Erikson's Theory to a Client System
scenarios.scenario: Margaret is a 72-year-old retired teacher who was referred to your senior services agency by her physician. Since her husband's death 18 months ago, Margaret has become increasingly isolated. She stopped volunteering at the library, rarely sees her grandchildren, and spends most days watching television. She tells you, 'I look back at my life and wonder if any of it mattered. I was just a teacher. I never did anything important.' She also expresses regret about not traveling more and not reconciling with her estranged sister before her sister's death 5 years ago. Despite this, Margaret occasionally mentions proud moments — a former student who became a doctor and credited Margaret, her 45-year marriage, and raising three children who 'turned out well.'
scenarios.writing_prompt: Write an analysis that includes: (1) Identify Margaret's current psychosocial stage and explain its relevance, (2) Analyze how grief is affecting her developmental task, (3) Apply at least two additional theories (e.g., Kübler-Ross, attachment theory, activity theory), (4) Develop an intervention plan grounded in theory, (5) Discuss how you would use the strengths perspective.

erikson.criteria: Correctly identifies Integrity vs. Despair, explains the stage thoroughly, connects Margaret's statements to stage-specific behaviors

grief_analysis.criteria: Analyzes how unresolved grief (husband, sister) is complicating the developmental task, discusses complicated grief indicators

additional_theories.criteria: Meaningfully applies 2+ theories beyond Erikson with specific connections to Margaret's situation

intervention.criteria: Theory-grounded plan: life review/reminiscence therapy, grief counseling, gradual reengagement, explore meaning-making

strengths.criteria: Identifies specific strengths: teaching legacy, long marriage, successful children, prior community involvement, willingness to attend session

model_response_key_points: Correctly identify Integrity vs. Despair (Stage 8)
Explain that this stage involves reflecting on life with acceptance or regret
Connect Margaret's 'it didn't matter' statements to despair
Connect her proud moments to potential for integrity
Analyze grief's impact on her ability to resolve this stage
Apply additional theories meaningfully
Recommend life review/reminiscence therapy
Use strengths perspective to reframe her narrative
scenarios.tips: Erikson's stages are heavily tested on the ASWB exam. Know each stage, the age range, the central conflict, and what successful vs. unsuccessful resolution looks like. Pay special attention to Stages 5-8 as these are most commonly tested.

scenarios.title: Cultural Competence in Practice
scenarios.scenario: You are assigned to work with the Nguyen family, who immigrated from Vietnam 5 years ago. The parents, Thanh (father, 48) and Linh (mother, 44), are concerned about their 16-year-old daughter, Mai, who has been 'acting American' — wanting to date, refusing to speak Vietnamese at home, and arguing about attending college instead of working in the family restaurant. Thanh tells you through an interpreter (his 12-year-old son, Bao) that Mai is 'disrespecting the family' and needs to 'remember who she is.' Mai tells you privately that she feels torn between two worlds and has been having panic attacks. She also shares that she is questioning her sexual orientation but begs you not to tell her parents, saying 'they would disown me.'
scenarios.writing_prompt: Write a comprehensive cultural assessment and intervention plan addressing: (1) Multiple cultural dynamics at play, (2) Ethical concerns with using the child as interpreter, (3) How you would navigate Mai's confidentiality regarding sexual orientation, (4) Culturally responsive intervention strategies, (5) Your own cultural humility and potential biases.

cultural_dynamics.criteria: Identifies: acculturation gap, individualist vs collectivist values, generational conflict, bicultural identity stress, intersectionality of ethnicity, gender, and potential LGBTQ+ identity

interpreter_ethics.criteria: Identifies ethical problem of child interpreter (parentification, power dynamics, NASW standards), proposes professional interpreter

confidentiality.criteria: Navigates the complex tension between Mai's right to confidentiality about sexual orientation, safety assessment, family's cultural values, and minor's rights by state law

interventions.criteria: Culturally responsive: respects family hierarchy while supporting Mai's development, explores family's immigration experience, connects to Vietnamese community resources, addresses panic attacks, considers family therapy with cultural adaptation

self_awareness.criteria: Demonstrates cultural humility, acknowledges potential Western bias, considers own positionality, commits to ongoing learning about Vietnamese culture

model_response_key_points: Never use a family member (especially a child) as an interpreter
Acknowledge and respect the family's cultural values while supporting Mai
Recognize acculturation stress as a normative experience, not pathology
Navigate confidentiality carefully — Mai's safety is the deciding factor
Use an intersectional lens to understand Mai's multiple identities
Explore your own cultural positionality and potential biases
Connect the family to culturally specific resources
Avoid imposing Western individualistic values
scenarios.tips: Cultural competence questions on the licensing exam often test whether you can balance cultural respect with professional ethics. The answer is rarely 'impose your values' OR 'defer entirely to culture.' Look for the response that demonstrates cultural humility while upholding professional standards.

scenarios.title: Policy Analysis — Housing First Model
scenarios.scenario: Your agency has been asked to provide testimony to the city council regarding the adoption of a Housing First model for addressing chronic homelessness. The council is divided: some members support traditional shelter-based approaches that require sobriety and program compliance, while others want to adopt Housing First. Your agency serves approximately 200 chronically homeless individuals, 65% of whom have co-occurring mental health and substance use disorders. The current shelter system has a 23% success rate in transitioning clients to permanent housing. The city has allocated $2 million for homelessness services next fiscal year.
scenarios.writing_prompt: Write a formal policy analysis brief that includes: (1) Problem statement with relevant data, (2) Analysis of both policy options using a social work policy framework, (3) Evidence base for each approach, (4) Cost-benefit analysis, (5) Equity and social justice implications, (6) Your recommendation with implementation plan.

problem_statement.criteria: Clear definition of chronic homelessness, local data, population characteristics, current system limitations

policy_analysis.criteria: Uses established framework (e.g., Popple & Leighninger, Gilbert & Terrell), analyzes both options across dimensions of adequacy, equity, efficiency

evidence_base.criteria: Cites Housing First research (Tsemberis, Pathways to Housing), shelter-based outcomes, cost studies, comparative effectiveness data

cost_benefit.criteria: Compares costs of both models, includes indirect costs (ER visits, incarceration, shelter cycling), discusses ROI

equity.criteria: Analyzes racial disparities in homelessness, barriers in treatment-first model, dignity and self-determination

recommendation.criteria: Clear recommendation with phased implementation plan, measurable outcomes, accountability mechanisms

model_response_key_points: Frame the analysis using a recognized social work policy framework
Present both sides fairly but support recommendation with evidence
Include specific research citations for Housing First outcomes
Address cost-effectiveness (Housing First typically costs less long-term)
Discuss equity and racial disparities in homelessness
Connect to social work values of dignity and self-determination
Propose a realistic implementation plan
Include measurable outcomes and evaluation plan
scenarios.tips: Policy questions on the licensing exam test your ability to think at the macro level. Know major social welfare policies and programs, understand how to analyze policy from a social work perspective, and always connect policy to social work values.

scenarios.title: Program Evaluation Design
scenarios.scenario: Your agency has been running a 12-week group therapy program for survivors of domestic violence for the past two years. The program includes psychoeducation, CBT-based coping skills, safety planning, and peer support. The agency director wants to apply for a federal grant to expand the program but needs evidence of effectiveness. You have been asked to design a program evaluation. The program currently serves approximately 40 participants per year across 4 groups. You have access to pre/post assessments that have been administered (but not analyzed), attendance records, and client satisfaction surveys.
scenarios.writing_prompt: Design a comprehensive program evaluation proposal that includes: (1) A logic model for the program, (2) Research questions, (3) Evaluation design and methodology, (4) Outcome measures and data collection plan, (5) Data analysis plan, (6) Ethical considerations, (7) Limitations and strategies to address them.

logic_model.criteria: Clear inputs, activities, outputs, short-term and long-term outcomes; logical connections between elements

research_questions.criteria: Specific, measurable questions about program effectiveness, client satisfaction, and implementation fidelity

design.criteria: Appropriate design (pre-post, quasi-experimental if possible), addresses internal validity threats, considers comparison group

measures.criteria: Valid and reliable outcome measures (e.g., PCL-5, BDI-II, Danger Assessment), process measures, cultural appropriateness of tools

analysis.criteria: Appropriate statistical methods (paired t-tests, repeated measures ANOVA), qualitative component, effect sizes

ethics.criteria: IRB considerations, informed consent with vulnerable population, confidentiality in group setting, trauma-sensitive research practices

limitations.criteria: Identifies threats (small sample, no random assignment, attrition) and proposes mitigation strategies

model_response_key_points: Create a clear logic model connecting program components to outcomes
Use validated outcome measures appropriate for the population
Address the ethical complexities of research with DV survivors
Consider both quantitative and qualitative methods
Acknowledge limitations honestly while proposing mitigation
Connect evaluation back to evidence-based practice
Consider cultural responsiveness of assessment tools
Plan for participant attrition in the design
scenarios.tips: Research questions on the licensing exam focus on basic research literacy: understanding designs, validity threats, ethical requirements (especially informed consent and IRB), and the difference between qualitative and quantitative methods.

scenarios.title: Differential Diagnosis and Treatment Planning
scenarios.scenario: Aisha (age 35) presents with the following symptoms over the past 3 months: persistent sadness, difficulty sleeping (waking at 3 AM nightly), loss of appetite (12-pound weight loss), difficulty concentrating at work, and withdrawal from friends. She reports a traumatic event 4 months ago when she witnessed a violent robbery at her workplace. Since then, she has nightmares about the event 3-4 times per week, avoids the area where it occurred (which means taking a longer route to work), startles easily at loud noises, and reports feeling 'constantly on edge.' Aisha also mentions that she has 'always been a worrier' and has had periodic episodes of intense anxiety with heart racing, shortness of breath, and dizziness lasting about 10 minutes since her early 20s. She drinks 2-3 glasses of wine nightly 'to calm down,' increased from occasional social drinking before the robbery.
scenarios.writing_prompt: Write a comprehensive clinical assessment that includes: (1) Differential diagnosis discussion analyzing each possible diagnosis with DSM-5 criteria, (2) Your recommended diagnoses with rationale, (3) Rule-out diagnoses, (4) A comprehensive treatment plan with evidence-based interventions prioritized, (5) Risk assessment regarding substance use.

differential.criteria: Systematically evaluates PTSD, MDD, Panic Disorder, GAD, Alcohol Use Disorder using DSM-5 criteria; distinguishes overlapping symptoms

diagnoses.criteria: Identifies primary PTSD (with all criteria clusters), comorbid MDD, Panic Disorder (pre-existing), and assesses Alcohol Use Disorder criteria

rule_outs.criteria: Rules out medical conditions, substance-induced symptoms, adjustment disorder, acute stress disorder (>1 month)

treatment_plan.criteria: Prioritizes evidence-based treatments: trauma-focused CBT or CPT/PE for PTSD, addresses substance use, considers pharmacological consultation, includes timeline and measurable goals

risk_assessment.criteria: Evaluates alcohol use pattern, self-medication, risk of escalation, screening for AUD, addresses substance use in treatment sequencing

model_response_key_points: Use DSM-5 criteria systematically for each potential diagnosis
Distinguish PTSD from depression (they share overlapping symptoms)
Note that panic attacks pre-date the trauma (separate panic disorder)
Assess alcohol use as potential AUD vs situational coping
Recommend trauma-focused evidence-based treatment (PE, CPT, or EMDR)
Address treatment sequencing — which issues to target first
Consider safety and stabilization before trauma processing
Include measurable treatment goals with timelines
scenarios.tips: The clinical licensing exam heavily tests differential diagnosis. Pay attention to timelines — when symptoms started relative to events matters for diagnosis. Comorbidity is the norm, not the exception, in clinical practice.

scenarios.title: Termination and Transfer Summary
scenarios.scenario: You have been working with Rosa (age 38) for 8 months at your community mental health center. Rosa was initially referred for severe depression and PTSD following a domestic violence experience. Over the course of treatment, Rosa has made significant progress: she obtained a protective order, moved to safe housing through a DV transitional program, began part-time employment, reconnected with her sister, and her PHQ-9 scores decreased from 24 to 8. You are leaving the agency for a new position and must transfer Rosa to a new clinician. Rosa has expressed anxiety about the transition, stating 'You're the only one who believed me. I don't want to start over with someone new.'
scenarios.writing_prompt: Write: (1) A comprehensive transfer summary document for the receiving clinician, (2) A session plan for how you would conduct the termination session with Rosa, (3) An analysis of the ethical considerations involved in termination and transfer.

transfer_summary.criteria: Complete clinical summary including: presenting problem, treatment history, diagnosis, interventions used, progress (with measurable outcomes), current functioning, remaining needs, medications, safety considerations, recommendations for continued treatment

session_plan.criteria: Addresses: processing feelings about ending, reviewing progress and growth, normalizing transition anxiety, introducing new clinician, providing closure, reinforcing coping skills, creating a transition plan with timeline

ethics.criteria: Discusses: NASW standards on termination (1.16), abandonment vs appropriate termination, managing dependency, client's attachment history and how it affects this transition, continuity of care, proper documentation, informed consent

model_response_key_points: Acknowledge and validate Rosa's feelings about the transition
Frame termination as part of the therapeutic process
Highlight Rosa's growth and attribute progress to her own strength
Prepare a thorough transfer summary for continuity
Plan a graduated transition (overlap sessions if possible)
Address attachment dynamics — this may trigger trauma responses
Ensure proper documentation protects both client and clinician
Connect termination process to Rosa's larger healing narrative
scenarios.tips: Termination is a frequently tested topic. Remember that planned termination should be gradual, collaborative, and clinically processed. The way you end treatment matters as much as how you begin it.

scenarios.title: Social Media and Professional Boundaries
scenarios.scenario: You are a school social worker working with high school students. One of your clients, Tyler (age 17), sends you a friend request on Instagram. You do not accept it, but you notice his profile is public. Out of concern for his wellbeing (he recently disclosed self-harm), you view his profile and discover posts suggesting he is using drugs, attending parties, and making concerning statements about 'not being here much longer.' Some posts include other students you also work with. Your supervisor suggests you screen all student clients' social media regularly 'for safety purposes.'
scenarios.writing_prompt: Write an analysis that addresses: (1) Ethical issues with accepting the friend request, (2) Ethical issues with viewing the public profile, (3) Whether and how the social media content should be addressed clinically, (4) Your response to the supervisor's suggestion, (5) A proposed social media policy for your school social work practice.

friend_request.criteria: Clear explanation of why accepting is inappropriate (dual relationship, boundary violation, power dynamics with minor)

viewing_profile.criteria: Nuanced analysis: public profile vs privacy expectations, whether viewing constitutes surveillance, informed consent issues, how viewing changes the therapeutic relationship

clinical_response.criteria: Discusses: duty to respond to safety concerns, how to bring up observations without revealing source in a way that damages trust, considers Tyler's autonomy, addresses clinically not punitively

supervisor_response.criteria: Respectfully pushes back with ethical rationale, discusses systematic screening as surveillance vs individual safety concerns, proposes alternative approaches

policy_proposal.criteria: Comprehensive social media policy addressing: general prohibition on connecting with clients, guidelines for incidental discovery, documentation requirements, consent considerations, training for staff

model_response_key_points: Declining the friend request was correct — explain why with ethical codes
Viewing public profiles is ethically gray — discuss the complexity
Safety concerns may justify action but process matters
Address the content therapeutically, not punitively
Systematic social media surveillance is ethically problematic
Propose transparent policies that balance safety and privacy
Consider the developmental needs of adolescents online
Document your reasoning process
scenarios.tips: Digital ethics is an emerging area in social work. While licensing exams may not ask specifically about social media, the underlying principles (boundaries, confidentiality, dual relationships, informed consent) are tested constantly. Apply traditional ethics to digital contexts.

Psychotherapy, Clinical Interventions, and Case Management

soap_note.name: SOAP Note Format
soap_note.description: Standard progress note format used in clinical settings

biopsychosocial.name: Biopsychosocial Assessment
biopsychosocial.description: Comprehensive assessment framework examining biological, psychological, and social dimensions

treatment_plan.name: Treatment Plan
treatment_plan.description: Structured plan for addressing identified client needs

process_recording.name: Process Recording
process_recording.description: Detailed recording of a practice interaction for supervision and learning

policy_brief.name: Policy Analysis Brief
policy_brief.description: Formal analysis of social policy for advocacy and decision-making

case_presentation.name: Case Presentation
case_presentation.description: Structured format for presenting cases in supervision or team meetings

nasw_code_sections.summary: Social workers' primary responsibility is to promote well-being of clients
Respect and promote clients' right to self-determination
Provide services with valid informed consent
Be alert to and avoid conflicts of interest
Terminate services when no longer needed or serving client's needs
Engage in social and political action

defense_mechanisms.name: Denial
defense_mechanisms.example: A person with alcohol use disorder insisting they can stop anytime

defense_mechanisms.name: Projection
defense_mechanisms.example: An angry client accusing the social worker of being hostile

defense_mechanisms.name: Displacement
defense_mechanisms.description: Redirecting emotions to a safer target
defense_mechanisms.example: A client who is angry at their boss yelling at their children instead

defense_mechanisms.name: Rationalization
defense_mechanisms.description: Creating logical explanations for irrational behavior
defense_mechanisms.example: A parent who hits their child saying 'I turned out fine'

defense_mechanisms.name: Sublimation
defense_mechanisms.description: Channeling unacceptable impulses into acceptable activities
defense_mechanisms.example: A person with aggressive tendencies becoming a competitive athlete

defense_mechanisms.name: Regression
defense_mechanisms.example: An adult throwing a tantrum when frustrated

defense_mechanisms.name: Intellectualization
defense_mechanisms.description: Using abstract thinking to avoid emotions
defense_mechanisms.example: A grieving person discussing the 'stages of grief' without expressing feelings
